caveats: a: The BLS source is four-digit nonscheduled air transportation and includes passenger charter and specialty flying.
a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and is a 2023 occupational snapshot.
a: Task exposure is researcher judgment, not a measured AI adoption or automation statistic.
rho: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation in nonscheduled cargo charter.
rho: The estimate excludes autonomous-aircraft displacement and counts only implementation of the task opportunity in a.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may include entities whose economics or certificate status do not match the lens.
e: No public dataset identifies repeat external revenue at the firm level.
s5: Gallup measures intentions, not completed qualifying transfers.
s5: The survey population spans industries and business sizes and may not represent asset-heavy air carriers.
q: The SEC filing is from a much larger operator than the firms in the lens.
q: Commercial terms vary sharply between spot charter, government flying, CMI, and ACMI.
d5: FAA's forecast combines scheduled and nonscheduled cargo and passenger-belly freight.
d5: The forecast is model-based and sensitive to GDP, trade, tariff, fuel-price, and modal-share assumptions.
o: Future BVLOS and autonomous-aircraft regulation may change operator staffing and certificate structures.
o: The estimate distinguishes operator requirement from employment intensity; automation may reduce labor without eliminating the operator.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy aircraft capacity by trip or block hour under full-service charter, CMI, or ACMI arrangements; repeat programs can guarantee flying, while spot charters are transaction-based. Fuel and other direct costs may be borne by the customer under ACMI or included in a fixed charter price.
lens.excluded_activities: Scheduled freight carriers, passenger charter, air couriers, aircraft-only lessors, captive internal flight departments, inactive certificate shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market certificated air carriers providing recurring or repeat outsourced, nonscheduled cargo-only charter, CMI, or ACMI flying to freight forwarders, integrators, government, airlines, and direct shippers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 2023 occupation and wage mix for four-digit NAICS 481200, not cargo-only 481212. I grouped office, finance, customer-service, sales, logistics, dispatch, planning, compliance-documentation, and a limited share of manager, pilot, and maintenance paperwork/diagnostic tasks; applied materially lower task exposure to safety-critical flying, hands-on maintenance, and material handling; and translated the result into a wage-weighted exposed-task share. Inclusion of passenger-charter flight attendants and a potentially different cargo-firm mix can bias the proxy either way, while treating whole occupations as only partially exposed limits upward bias.
caveats: The BLS source is four-digit nonscheduled air transportation and includes passenger charter and specialty flying.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers and is a 2023 occupational snapshot.
Task exposure is researcher judgment, not a measured AI adoption or automation statistic.
a.change_evidence: A payroll extract from 10-15 cargo-only Part 135/121 charter operators mapped to detailed roles, combined with time-and-motion sampling of quoting, dispatch, maintenance records, compliance, billing, and customer-service tasks, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is operationally heavy: commercial pilots were 33.84% of employment and aircraft mechanics 12.02%, while office and administrative support was 13.55%, business and financial operations 6.82%, and management 7.74%. AI can materially reduce quoting, customer correspondence, document review, bookkeeping, scheduling support, maintenance-record work, and dispatch preparation, but it does not substitute for most flight, wrench-turning, inspection, or loading labor within five years.

d5.bridge: FAA forecasts total US commercial-air-carrier cargo RTMs, including freight/express and mail on scheduled and nonscheduled services, from 50.427 billion in 2026 to 59.934 billion in 2031, a 1.188 ratio. I haircut the center to 1.16 for the narrower nonscheduled charter basket and bracket downside from modal competition, tariffs, and customer insourcing and upside from time-critical, irregular, defense, and feeder demand. RTMs are transport output rather than constant-quality charter-service quantity and may overstate demand if average haul length rises.
caveats: FAA's forecast combines scheduled and nonscheduled cargo and passenger-belly freight.
The forecast is model-based and sensitive to GDP, trade, tariff, fuel-price, and modal-share assumptions.
d5.change_evidence: DOT T-100 segment data isolating nonscheduled all-cargo departures, block hours, payload, and RTMs by carrier through 2031, deflated charter-rate histories, and contracted customer capacity plans would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: The broad air-cargo outlook is positive, and FAA expects all-cargo capacity to retain a dominant share, but charter freight is more cyclical and event-driven than the aggregate. A modestly lower five-year base than the aggregate RTM ratio is therefore appropriate.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may include entities whose economics or certificate status do not match the lens.
No public dataset identifies repeat external revenue at the firm level.
e.change_evidence: An FAA certificate-by-certificate roster joined to DOT authority, active aircraft, customer concentration, contract tenure, and related-party revenue for all firms in the modeled EBITDA band would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is external cargo transport, and FAA describes Part 135 operators ranging from one aircraft to large cargo feeders, so much of the population fits outsourced service. Eligibility is reduced for one-off-only spot operators, inactive certificate shells, captive operations, businesses without repeat customers, and entities whose value is primarily aircraft ownership rather than transferable operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 481212 ($1,275,515,932) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 481212 ($6,386,048,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1027 (margins.json[481]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Air Transport EBITDA/Sales 10.27%); revenue/employee $706,656 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 481212); class <5: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,428,777 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $146,735 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,668,917 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $274,098 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,521,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,183,298 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,416,263 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,226,450 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $112,485,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,552,280 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $307,276,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,557,245 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 28

caveats: Future BVLOS and autonomous-aircraft regulation may change operator staffing and certificate structures.
The estimate distinguishes operator requirement from employment intensity; automation may reduce labor without eliminating the operator.
o.change_evidence: FAA Part 135 UAS operating data, the final BVLOS/Part 108 rule and associated staffing requirements, autonomous cargo aircraft certifications, and customer insourcing plans would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Physical cargo transport cannot be delivered by software alone. Even unmanned package delivery beyond visual line of sight currently requires a Part 135 operator certificate, airspace authorization, operating procedures, and accountable safety management. Some light feeder or medical cargo can migrate to new drone/AAM operators, and integrators can internalize flying, but most year-five charter quantity should still require an accountable air-carrier operator.

caveats: The SEC filing is from a much larger operator than the firms in the lens.
Commercial terms vary sharply between spot charter, government flying, CMI, and ACMI.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract sample showing duration, escalation, open-book cost clauses, productivity-sharing, renewal outcomes, and customer concentration, plus bid histories before and after workflow automation, would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: ACMI/CMI arrangements commonly charge predetermined operating rates while customers bear fuel and some direct expenses, allowing near-term labor productivity to accrue to the operator. Full-service charter is often a fixed all-inclusive price, also supporting retention until repricing. Over renewals, sophisticated integrators, freight forwarders, and government customers can demand lower rates or share gains, and bidding competition erodes retention, so the discounted five-year retained share is materially below one.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation in nonscheduled cargo charter.
The estimate excludes autonomous-aircraft displacement and counts only implementation of the task opportunity in a.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before/after labor hours from AI deployments at Part 135 cargo carriers, FAA findings involving AI-assisted operational workflows, and implementation interviews with directors of operations, chief pilots, maintenance leaders, and principal inspectors would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Document-heavy commercial and administrative workflows can be deployed with human review, but flight operations, maintenance release, hazardous-materials programs, manuals, training, and operational control remain safety-regulated and accountable. Fragmented fleets and low-volume edge cases also slow integration, so only about half of the exposed task opportunity is likely to become operating reality within five years.

s5.bridge: Gallup asked US employer-business owners across industries about plans for the next five years and found 22% planned to sell or transfer ownership. I use that as the center for eligible charter-freight firms, with a wide range for aviation-specific owner age, capital intensity, certificate transfer friction, strategic consolidation, and the gap between stated plans and completed qualifying control transfers. The proxy may overstate completed transfers because plans can lapse or become closures, but industry consolidation and scarce operating certificates may bias it upward.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions, not completed qualifying transfers.
The survey population spans industries and business sizes and may not represent asset-heavy air carriers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed control transactions from aviation M&A databases and FAA/DOT certificate-control filings, matched to owner age and firm size, would replace the cross-industry proxy.
s5.rationale: A planned five-year sale-or-transfer rate is the closest directly observed horizon match, but it is not aviation-specific and not every planned transaction will close or transfer an operating platform. Aircraft, customer contracts, management qualifications, and regulatory approvals make these firms less fungible than typical small employers.

principal_driver: Repeat outsourced cargo programs create a durable physical service base where AI can compress document- and coordination-heavy overhead.
principal_weakness: Most wage cost sits in regulated, safety-critical flight and maintenance work that AI is unlikely to substitute materially within five years.
